title: Charmed Life — 3 (Fragment 1)
sourceText:
Chapter 3

3

T 
 HE TRAIN JOURNEY lasted about an hour, before the train puffed into Bowbridge, where they were to get out.

“It’s frightfully small,” Gwendolen said critically.

“Bowbridge!” shouted a porter, running along the platform. “Bowbridge. The young Chants alight here, please.”

“Young Chants!” Gwendolen said disdainfully. “Can’t they treat me with more respect?” All the same, the attention pleased her. Cat could see that, as she drew on her ladylike gloves, she was shaking with excitement. He cowered behind her as they got out and watched their trunks being tossed out onto the windy platform. Gwendolen marched up to the shouting porter. “ We are the young Chants,” she told him magnificently.

It fell a little flat. The porter simply beckoned and scurried away to the entrance lobby, which was windier even than the platform. Gwendolen had to hold her hat on. Here, a young man strode towards them in a billow of flapping coat.

“ We are the young Chants,” Gwendolen told him.

“Gwendolen and Eric? Pleased to meet you,” said the young man. “I’m Michael Saunders. I’ll be tutoring you with the other children.”

“ Other children?” Gwendolen asked him haughtily. But Mr. Saunders was evidently one of those people who are not good at standing still. He had already darted off to see about their trunks. Gwendolen was a trifle annoyed. But when Mr. Saunders came back and led them outside into the station yard, they found a motor car waiting—long, black, and sleek. Gwendolen forgot her annoyance. She felt this was entirely fitting.

Cat wished it had been a carriage. The car jerked and thrummed and smelled of petrol. He felt sick almost at once. He felt sicker still when they left Bowbridge and thrummed along a winding country road. The only advantage he could see was that the car went very quickly. After only ten minutes, Mr. Saunders said, “Look—there’s Chrestomanci Castle now. You get the best view from here.”

Cat turned his sick face and Gwendolen her fresh one the way he pointed. The Castle was gray and turreted, on the opposite hill. As the road turned, they saw it had a new part, with a spread of big windows, and a flag flying above. They could see grand trees—dark, layered cedars and big elms—and glimpse lawns and flowers.

“It looks marvelous,” Cat said sickly, rather surprised that Gwendolen had said nothing. He hoped the road did not wind too much in getting to the Castle.

It did not. The car flashed around a village green and between big gates. Then there was a long tree-lined avenue, with the great door of the old part of the Castle at the end of it. The car scrunched around on the gravel sweep in front of it. Gwendolen leaned forward eagerly, ready to be first one out. It was clear there would be a butler, and perhaps footmen too. She could hardly wait to make her grand entry.

But the car went on, past the gray, knobbly walls of the old Castle, and stopped at an obscure door where the new part began. It was almost a secretive door. There was a mass of rhododendron trees hiding it from both parts of the Castle.

words.definition: To emit smoke or steam, as a train does when arriving.
words.example: The train puffed into Bowbridge, where they were to get out.
words.exampleText: The train puffed into Bowbridge, where they were to get out.

words.definition: In a way that expresses disapproval or judgement.
words.example: “It’s frightfully small,” Gwendolen said critically.
words.exampleText: “It’s frightfully small,” Gwendolen said critically.

words.definition: In a manner that shows contempt or lack of respect.
words.example: “Young Chants!” Gwendolen said disdainfully.
words.exampleText: “Young Chants!” Gwendolen said disdainfully.

words.definition: Having qualities or manners regarded as typical of a well-bred woman.
words.example: She drew on her ladylike gloves.
words.exampleText: She drew on her ladylike gloves.

words.definition: To crouch down in fear.
words.example: He cowered behind her as they got out.
words.exampleText: He cowered behind her as they got out.

words.definition: Large, strong cases used for carrying clothes and belongings.
words.example: They watched their trunks being tossed out onto the platform.
words.exampleText: They watched their trunks being tossed out onto the platform.

words.definition: To walk with regular, measured steps often in a determined or confident manner.
words.example: Gwendolen marched up to the shouting porter.
words.exampleText: Gwendolen marched up to the shouting porter.

words.definition: To make a gesture with the hand to encourage someone to come nearer or follow.
words.example: The porter simply beckoned and scurried away.
words.exampleText: The porter simply beckoned and scurried away.

words.definition: To move quickly with short, hurried movements.
words.example: He scurried away to the entrance lobby.
words.exampleText: He scurried away to the entrance lobby.

words.definition: A large undulating mass, such as of smoke or fabric.
words.example: A young man strode towards them in a billow of flapping coat.
words.exampleText: A young man strode towards them in a billow of flapping coat.

words.definition: To walk with long, decisive steps.
words.example: He strode towards them confidently.
words.exampleText: He strode towards them confidently.

words.definition: To give individual instruction to someone.
words.example: I’ll be tutoring you with the other children.
words.exampleText: I’ll be tutoring you with the other children.

words.definition: In a manner showing disdainful pride or arrogance.
words.example: Gwendolen asked him haughtily.
words.exampleText: Gwendolen asked him haughtily.

words.definition: To move suddenly and quickly in a particular direction.
words.example: Mr. Saunders had already darted off to see about their trunks.
words.exampleText: Mr. Saunders had already darted off to see about their trunks.

words.definition: Feeling slightly angry or irritated.
words.example: Gwendolen was a trifle annoyed.
words.exampleText: Gwendolen was a trifle annoyed.

words.definition: Appropriate or suitable to the situation.
words.example: She felt this was entirely fitting.
words.exampleText: She felt this was entirely fitting.

words.definition: To make a sudden, sharp movement.
words.example: The car jerked and thrummed.
words.exampleText: The car jerked and thrummed.

words.definition: To make a continuous rhythmic humming sound.
words.example: The car jerked and thrummed and smelled of petrol.
words.exampleText: The car jerked and thrummed and smelled of petrol.

words.definition: Looking or feeling unhealthy or ill.
words.example: He said it looks marvelous, said sickly.
words.exampleText: He said it looks marvelous, said sickly.

words.definition: Having small towers or turrets (architectural features).
words.example: The Castle was gray and turreted.
words.exampleText: The Castle was gray and turreted.

words.definition: To see something briefly or partially.
words.example: Glimpse lawns and flowers.
words.exampleText: Glimpse lawns and flowers.

words.definition: Causing wonder; extraordinarily good or great.
words.example: It looks marvelous.
words.exampleText: It looks marvelous.

words.definition: A common open area within a village, often grassy.
words.example: The car flashed around a village green.
words.exampleText: The car flashed around a village green.

words.definition: A wide street or a pathway lined with trees.
words.example: There was a long tree-lined avenue.
words.exampleText: There was a long tree-lined avenue.

words.definition: To make a crunching noise or movement.
words.example: The car scrunched around on the gravel sweep.
words.exampleText: The car scrunched around on the gravel sweep.

words.definition: With keen or enthusiastic desire or interest.
words.example: Gwendolen leaned forward eagerly.
words.exampleText: Gwendolen leaned forward eagerly.

words.definition: A manservant in charge of the household staff.
words.example: There would be a butler, and perhaps footmen too.
words.exampleText: There would be a butler, and perhaps footmen too.

words.definition: Not clearly seen or easily distinguished; hidden.
words.example: The car stopped at an obscure door.
words.exampleText: The car stopped at an obscure door.

words.definition: Inclined to conceal feelings and intentions or not to disclose information.
words.example: It was almost a secretive door.
words.exampleText: It was almost a secretive door.

words.definition: A type of flowering shrub with large clusters of bright flowers.
words.example: There was a mass of rhododendron trees hiding the door.
words.exampleText: There was a mass of rhododendron trees hiding the door.
